risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "This randomized controlled trial was conducted in Shiraz... Seventy caregivers patients with schizophrenia whose records were available at three psychiatric centers in Shiraz were randomized blindly to two groups..." and "Therefore, seventy caregivers of outpatients with a diagnosis of schizophrenia disorder who were members of their immediate family were randomly allocated to the experimental (n = 35) or control group (n = 35)." Comment: The paper states that participants were randomized/randomly allocated, but it does not describe how the allocation sequence was generated (e.g. computer-generated list, random number table, blocks, stratification). There is also no explicit non-random method reported. Therefore the sequence generation method is insufficiently described and the risk is unclear.

Quote: "Seventy caregivers patients with schizophrenia whose records were available at three psychiatric centers in Shiraz were randomized blindly to two groups..." and "therefore, seventy caregivers of outpatients... were randomly allocated to the experimental (n = 35) or control group (n = 35)." Comment: The report states that participants were randomized/blindly allocated, but it does not explicitly describe any allocation concealment mechanism (e.g. central randomization, pharmacy-controlled allocation, or sequentially numbered opaque sealed envelopes). 'Randomized blindly' does not establish that the person enrolling participants could not foresee the upcoming assignment.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "In the control group, only the patients received routine care (antipsychotic drug treatment) whereas in the experimental group, the caregivers participated in a psycho-educational program while the patients received antipsychotic drug treatment." and "The intervention was conducted by the same psychiatrist and psychiatric nurse." Comment: The nature of the intervention means caregivers and treating personnel delivering the psycho-educational sessions would know group assignment; blinding of participants/personnel was not feasible. This could plausibly affect key outcomes because family burden was measured by questionnaire and patient psychiatric symptoms were "completed for every patient by caregiver," making outcomes susceptible to influence from knowledge of allocation.

Quote: "The co-researcher (assessor) was blind to study treatment and condition and completed the scales." and "Data were collected with ... the Iranian version of the Brief Psychiatric Rating Scale (BPRS) [29] which was completed for every patient by caregiver, and the Family Burden questionnaire [25]." Comment: Outcomes were assessed by a mix of assessors: an independent/blinded assessor completed scales, but key outcomes included caregiver-completed measures, notably the Family Burden questionnaire and caregiver-completed BPRS data. These are subjective/judgement-dependent outcomes, and caregivers in the intervention arm were necessarily aware they received psychoeducation. By the worst-outcome rule, the presence of unblinded patient/family self-report or proxy-report subjective outcomes makes detection bias high overall, despite the statement that a co-researcher assessor was blinded.

Quote: "A total of 65 families completed the study. Five participants (two from the experimental group and three from the control group) dropped out before completion of the study for different reasons unrelated to the study." And Table 2 reports analysed numbers: "Experimental group (N=33)" and "Control group (N=32) Mean". Comment: 70 were randomized (35/35), and 65 were analysed at post-intervention and 1-month follow-up (33/32), giving attrition of 2/35 (5.7%) vs 3/35 (8.6%). Loss was low, balanced between arms, over a short follow-up, and reasons were reported as unrelated to the study, so missing outcome data are unlikely to bias the results materially.
